name: Florence
localTips: Book the Uffizi and Accademia online days ahead — walk-up lines regularly run two to three hours, and timed-entry tickets cost a few euros more but save most of a morning.
The historic center is a restricted traffic zone (ZTL) — if you're driving in, don't enter the center without a permit; rental cars get fined automatically by camera.
Bistecca alla fiorentina is sold by weight (usually for two) and served rare by default — if you want it more done, say so when ordering, and expect to share one large steak.
Look for small wooden 'wine windows' (buchette del vino) built into building façades — reopened since the pandemic for a quick glass of wine handed straight through the wall.
August is when much of the city empties out for holiday — some family-run trattorias and shops close for two to three weeks, so check ahead if visiting in high summer.

activities.title: Piazza del Duomo
activities.description: Brunelleschi's terracotta-tiled dome, Giotto's striped marble bell tower and the octagonal Baptistery all face off across one square — free to walk around and crane your neck at.

activities.title: Piazzale Michelangelo at sunset
activities.description: A hilltop terrace across the Arno with the single best panorama of Florence — the Duomo, Palazzo Vecchio and the whole terracotta skyline laid out below a bronze copy of the David.

activities.title: Ponte Vecchio
activities.description: The only Florence bridge to survive WWII bombing, its shops overhanging the Arno have sold gold and jewelry since the 16th century — free to cross, best photographed from the next bridge over.

activities.title: Piazza della Signoria & Loggia dei Lanzi
activities.description: An open-air sculpture museum — a copy of the David, Cellini's bronze Perseus and the Rape of the Sabine Women stand free under the Loggia's arches, next to Palazzo Vecchio's tower.

activities.title: San Miniato al Monte
activities.description: A green-and-white marble Romanesque church above Piazzale Michelangelo with a glittering gold mosaic façade — free to enter, quieter than the Duomo, and arguably a better view.

activities.title: Oltrarno artisan workshops
activities.description: Leatherworkers, gilders and violin-makers still work behind open shop doors on the 'other side' of the Arno — Florence's living craft tradition, free to watch through the doorway.

activities.title: Mercato Centrale, San Lorenzo
activities.description: A cast-iron 1874 market hall — the ground floor's butchers, cheesemongers and produce stalls are free to browse, while the upstairs food hall stays open into the evening.

activities.title: San Lorenzo street market
activities.description: Leather jackets, scarves and souvenirs fill stalls that wrap around the Basilica di San Lorenzo — free to browse, and the fastest lesson in how to haggle politely in Florence.

activities.title: Giardino delle Rose
activities.description: A free terraced rose garden below Piazzale Michelangelo with a Yoko Ono sculpture garden inside it and the same skyline view without the crowds — over 1,000 rose varieties in bloom May–June.

activities.title: Piazza Santo Spirito
activities.description: An unpolished, un-touristy square in the Oltrarno with a plain Brunelleschi church façade and a morning market — where Florentines actually sit outside with a coffee or a spritz.

activities.title: Lungarno riverside walk
activities.description: The embankments along the Arno string together views of the Ponte Vecchio, Santa Trinita bridge and the hills beyond — quieter at either end of the historic center, especially at dusk.

activities.title: Mercato Nuovo & Il Porcellino
activities.description: A 16th-century loggia market with a bronze boar statue whose snout is rubbed shiny by visitors dropping a coin in its mouth for good luck — a straw-market tradition older than the statue itself.

activities.title: Forte di Belvedere ramparts
activities.description: A 16th-century Medici fortress on the hill above the Boboli Gardens — the outer ramparts and plaza are free to walk when no exhibition is running, with 360° views over the whole city.

activities.title: A glass through a wine window
activities.description: Small arched holes built into palazzo façades let Renaissance-era wine merchants sell flasks straight to the street, bypassing tax collectors — a handful have reopened as tiny to-go wine hatches.
activities.insiderNote: Babae in Santo Spirito and Osteria delle Brache both operate working wine windows — look for a small wooden shutter about knee height, often unmarked, and just ask.

activities.title: Biblioteca delle Oblate rooftop terrace
activities.description: A public library's café terrace two blocks from the Duomo with a free, near-eye-level view straight at the dome — locals do homework here while tourists queue for paid viewpoints nearby.
activities.insiderNote: Take the elevator to the top floor café — no ticket, no line, just order a coffee and grab a table on the terrace facing the Duomo.

activities.title: Panino at All'Antico Vinaio
activities.description: A tiny Via dei Neri sandwich counter with a queue that wraps the block — thick focaccia stuffed with cured meats and truffle cream, the single most photographed lunch in Florence.
activities.insiderNote: Order 'La Favolosa' if unsure, and go right at opening (around 10am) or after 3pm — midday the line can run 30–45 minutes.

activities.title: Bistecca alla fiorentina at a family trattoria
activities.description: A two-inch-thick T-bone from Chianina cattle, grilled rare over charcoal and sold by weight for the table — the definitive Florentine meal, best at an unglamorous neighborhood trattoria.
activities.insiderNote: It's priced per kilo (usually a minimum of ~1kg, serving two) and always served rare — ask for 'ben cotta' if you want it more done, though many kitchens will gently push back.

activities.title: Gelato at Vivoli
activities.description: Running since 1930 near Santa Croce and still churned in small batches, no towering swirls or neon colors — old-school Florentine gelato at the shop many locals still call the city's best.
activities.insiderNote: Order in a small cup (coppetta), not a cone — the texture is dense enough that a cone changes how it eats. Ask what's made that morning; flavors rotate daily.

activities.title: Wine flight at Le Volpi e l'Uva
activities.description: A closet-sized enoteca behind Ponte Vecchio pouring lesser-known Tuscan and Italian wines by the glass, chosen by owners who've run it since the 1990s — a locals' antidote to tourist wine bars.
activities.insiderNote: Ask the staff to build you a flight around a region you don't know — they clearly enjoy steering people away from the obvious Chianti-only list.

activities.title: Casa di Dante
activities.description: A small museum in the medieval lanes near the Duomo, built on the spot believed to be Dante Alighieri's childhood neighborhood — a quiet, uncrowded dive into the poet's Florence.
activities.insiderNote: It's easy to walk past — the entrance is on a narrow lane off Via Dante Alighieri with only a small plaque marking it, not a grand façade.

activities.title: Mercato delle Pulci flea market
activities.description: Piazza dei Ciompi's permanent flea market of antique furniture, vintage cameras and old postcards — a dealers' market where regulars still haggle, well off the main tourist grid.
activities.insiderNote: The last Sunday of the month brings extra outdoor stalls beyond the permanent shops — arrive early for the best pieces before other dealers pick them over.

activities.title: Belle Époque coffee at Caffè Gilli
activities.description: Pouring coffee since 1733 and in its current gilded, mirrored Piazza della Repubblica location since 1910 — Florence's grand café, a step back into 19th-century elegance.
activities.insiderNote: Order at the bar counter for the standard Italian espresso price — sitting at a table on the piazza multiplies the cost several times over.

activities.title: Aperitivo hour in the Oltrarno
activities.description: Order a Negroni or spritz at a Santo Spirito bar between 6 and 8:30pm and a free buffet of crostini, pasta and cheese typically appears with it — dinner-adjacent, and how Florence unwinds after work.
activities.insiderNote: The buffet is meant to accompany one drink per person, not be a stand-alone meal — order a second round if you're grazing generously, it's the polite unwritten rule.

activities.title: Uffizi Gallery
activities.description: Botticelli's Birth of Venus, Da Vinci's Annunciation and room after room of the Italian Renaissance in the Medici family's own former offices — book a timed slot well ahead.
activities.bookingQuery: Uffizi Gallery skip the line ticket Florence

activities.title: Accademia Gallery & Michelangelo's David
activities.description: The 17-foot marble David stands at the end of a long gallery of the sculptor's unfinished 'Prisoners' — one of the most famous statues on Earth, best seen with a pre-booked slot.

activities.title: Climb inside Brunelleschi's Dome
activities.description: 463 steps up between the dome's two shells, past the base of Vasari's Last Judgment fresco painted on the inner surface, to a narrow walkway ringing the lantern above the whole city.
activities.bookingQuery: Brunelleschi Dome climb ticket Florence Duomo

activities.title: Climb Giotto's Bell Tower
activities.description: 414 steps up the striped marble campanile — no dome to block the view, so the panorama actually includes Brunelleschi's dome itself alongside the skyline.
activities.bookingQuery: Giotto Bell Tower climb ticket Florence

activities.title: Basilica di Santa Croce
activities.description: Florence's pantheon — Michelangelo, Galileo and Machiavelli are all buried here, under a Gothic ceiling and Giotto frescoes, with a peaceful cloister designed by Brunelleschi out back.
activities.bookingQuery: Basilica di Santa Croce entry ticket Florence

activities.title: Palazzo Pitti & Boboli Gardens
activities.description: The Medici's later, grander home — Raphael-filled state rooms behind the façade, opening onto the Boboli Gardens' amphitheater, grottoes and terraced statuary climbing the hill behind.
activities.bookingQuery: Palazzo Pitti and Boboli Gardens ticket Florence

activities.title: Medici Chapels
activities.description: The Medici dynasty's opulent mausoleum, capped by Michelangelo's own sculpted tombs for Lorenzo and Giuliano — marble and semi-precious stone on a scale meant to rival any king's.

activities.title: Palazzo Vecchio tower & secret passages
activities.description: Climb the crenellated Arnolfo Tower for a rooftop-level view of the Duomo, or book the 'secret passages' tour through hidden staircases and a spy hole into the Salone dei Cinquecento.
activities.bookingQuery: Palazzo Vecchio tower and secret passages tour Florence

activities.title: Vasari Corridor guided tour
activities.description: A kilometer-long elevated passage the Medici built in 1565 so they could walk from Palazzo Vecchio to Palazzo Pitti without touching the street — reopened for small, tightly limited guided groups.
activities.bookingQuery: Vasari Corridor guided tour ticket Florence

activities.title: Chianti countryside wine tour
activities.description: A half-day out of the city into rolling vineyards and cypress-lined hills — cellar tastings at a family estate, usually paired with a Tuscan lunch of pici pasta and cured meats.
activities.bookingQuery: Chianti wine tour day trip from Florence

activities.title: Tuscan cooking class
activities.description: Hands-on pasta-making — rolling pici or tagliatelle by hand and learning a proper ragù — usually in a local's kitchen or a small cooking school in the Oltrarno, dinner included.
